fiction is as ontologically real as scientific models

its just different sign systems which address different problems and truths. you use different tools and languages to measure or work with them

you can create a model of the orbits around the sun which is completely wrong and tells you nothing, so it has no bearing on the world, and it gives you no transformative power or control. you can't stand at a certain place and see a dot in the sky that you knew would be there, you would see darkness.

similarly, you can tell stories which aren't true in a meaningful sense, and they also have no bearing on the world

but some stories do transform the world, how can something with no bearing on the world transform it?

Today's need is: I want to bring someone in and create a channel to onboard them (study their mind) and then decide where to put them. And when I'm done I don't need a record for this to exist anywhere. I think people are welcome to watch it & participate but there's no value in it long term "in the server" collective memory

Alternatively I can make a channel name that's just "onboarding" (ostensibly that's what the bottom floor is for) where I bring people in and calibrate them and then send them off to the correct floor 

And whoever I am currently onboarding can be in the channel description or a pinned message or something

The key for me is that someone needs to not want to cancel the space. That is like number one on the list. The entryist type who wants to revamp everything has gotten into ORI a few times.

I think the last blocker I need to solve is separating the "view" from the "raw text"

Like I need to be able to restructure where attention goes (of the collective), separate from how people are inputting things (which needs to remain stable for people to be able to rely on)
There is no open research institute without open research

messages.message:
I think you guys need to relax the "one video a day" thing. I think that's not the actual content. 

Like, this is hot: 

https://x.com/i/status/2076039983023157531
I think the actual thing is what's happening between you guys and the growing plurs there, and the videos should just be us getting a peak into what is happening to you guys & your minds
i am actually curious how everyone feels about the videos (especially people for whom it's not a "native" format)

this feels like a video that should go super viral. The message in it is beautiful and actionable and I feel like would relate to a ton of people 

https://x.com/TylerAlterman/status/2076064180797427739
thinking about how social media companies make ad hoc UIs to track what's happening "in the world". This would happen more if we could concentrate more of humanity's attention on specific events
i feel like a key part of what we're doing is giving people who don't have a voice/outlet for their research a good-faith incubator to take it to the next level
but like - in terms of onboarding - i hadn't even realized 'respect our norms in our space' was something which could be relevant in onboarding until you brought it up, and now it's obvious
so like 'onboarding' is also sorta 'vetting
Looking at Discord communities, the standard onboarding or orientation usually includes having to read and agree to rules.
The rules and structure are themselves technologies to impart on a participant
there's also the ability to have someone restate in own words, which helps
yeah this is what I look for. Saying "i agree" doesn't tell me anything (signing a contract doesn't tell me anything either about their willingness to follow it - I don't know if they understood it!)
https://x.com/fleshsimulator/status/2075987527354253406
In general, hermeneutics is only possible with some kind of memory mechanic. Without memory, there is only vibes.

remember requires choice? 

I don't like the "flat" remembrance of everything (dead mechanical archive)

the human minds I use for memory are discerning (they remember what matters, what is true, what is emotionally salient)
I think we keep coming back to this. The ORI archives are one thing, and the ORI organism is another thing. And they have a relationship
Actually banned ideas and their associated books
Here's my percentage true I put on each general idea
Would be interesting to have a list like this and add other load bearing epistemology and have attendees of the September event rate how much credence they give each
To figure out for different self identifying groups where the epistemic gaps are
Just because brain cells are not themselves minds and often distract from minds does not mean that we should seek to have less brain cells to unveil the mystery of the minds.

There is never flat remembrance because there is always the need to recall, even in reading. Every word read is in practice a mnemonic for the rest of the life of the reader.

There always has to be an operator, no matter how sophisticated the operation. The primary reason for disillusionment from complex operations is that one feels the operator is taken out of the picture, but this is false.

Having a desire to store *less* information and then citing a dislike for the "flat" remembrance of things matches pretty well to this mind of disillusionment. It really reads to me as, "I want the focus to be on what people consider true and salient, so I will make it harder to find the things that are not true and salient". Essentially trying to force the issue and require immediate creation of memory devices for a whole literature instead of just providing the literature.

The logical conclusion of this is indeed either a paradigm where lay Catholics were barred from reading the Bible (and instead had to rely on a mix of orality and basic literacy) or even a return to oral culture where things that are not relevant are forgotten in two generations. But this to me is anti-technological and even godless.

When I was trying to get The Syllabus off the ground, the idea was that some things which are always going to be there in the literature will just sometimes be forgotten books on a shelf. If someone ever saw a link to it in the future, they could still read it. But it might not be relevant for a long time; just that when it is, it is available. 

The only downside of this is if the storage of the book costs a lot of resources. With plaintext, this is not really an issue. Especially not with Discord which is practically made for storing community chat logs.

You are much better off just encouraging people to index the whole literature according to salience and then encouraging them to get high signal by looking to those indexes, rather than trying to do some clever ergodic tricks. Ergodicity could be interesting as an optional feature, but in the end it relies (like surprise parties) on ignorance and lack of imagination.

I point again to the parable of the wheat and the tares. The time for the tares to be separated from the wheat is upon the harvest, at the point of judgment. This happens when people read a document or look through a server or perform a search query. This does not happen at the level of a whole literature itself.

A published book is an interesting example where the point of judgment is before the actual text is assembled, where the totality of all writing is shaped into the "final" version. A published book is necessarily not an actively growing document. What makes ORI interesting is that it is actively growing. Someone could distill down ORI writings into books, but those books would not be ORI itself.

I also think that deciding to destroy or hide writings as a form of selection or to set into stone a judgment about what should and should not be read is only good if one lacks a vision of how others could benefit from the process notes in the future. There are many things in my Obsidian vault which were pretty much useless when I wrote them, but ended up being useful later. If I acted as the judge early, I myself would be under judgment.
It really does start and end with this: a platform should encourage more thriving, not encourage an aesthetic concern apart from thriving. (Aesthetics must let its followers thrive to be good.)
